title: AI for science | Sir Paul Nurse, Demis Hassabis, Jennifer Doudna, and John Jumper - YouTube
text:
HANNAH FRY: Hello, and welcome
to the Google DeepMind Podcast
with me, Professor Hannah Fry.
Now, those of you
who are watching
will notice that we are
not in the studio today,
and that is because we
are backstage at the AI
for Science Forum, a
very special event that
has been co-hosted between
the Royal Society and Google
DeepMind.
And as part of the day,
I had the opportunity
to speak to Demis Hassabis
on stage, to interview him,
and we thought that you might
like to hear that conversation.
So we have included it
here as a special episode.
And then even more
special than that,
as though one Nobel
laureate wasn't enough,
we have three further
Nobel laureates
joining me on stage for an
extended panel for you to enjoy.
[MUSIC PLAYING]
OK, now for the next
session, it is always
an absolute treat for me
and often a mind boggling
experience to get to interview
your co-host for today,
sir Demis Hassabis.
Now, Demis is a
computer scientist.
He's an Artificial
Intelligence researcher.
He's an entrepreneur
who co-founded DeepMind
in 2010, where he is still CEO.
Now, I don't know how much you
know about Demis' background.
Because as well as the Nobel
Prize, and the knighthood,
and being a fellow of the
Royal Society, and AlphaFold,
and AlphaGo, and having
150,000 citations,
he also had a previous life as a
very successful neuroscientist,
chess champion, and
video games designer.
I have to say, I have a
theory that, actually, Demis
worked out the secrets
to AGI a little while ago
and has been hiding it in his
basement and then just, like,
slowly eking out all of
these massive breakthroughs
one-by-one to the rest of us.
But one thing is for
sure, our conversations
are always genuinely
fascinating.
So please, welcome
Demis Hassabis.
Thank you.
[APPLAUSE]
Thank you, Demis.
Thank you so much.
OK, I heard that you didn't know
that the Nobel Prize was coming.
Is that true?
DEMIS HASSABIS: Yes, no--
HANNAH FRY: Really, really?
DEMIS HASSABIS: Yeah.
HANNAH FRY: How
did you find out?
DEMIS HASSABIS: Well, it was
quite a funny story, actually,
because we had heard some
rumor that our fold had
been sort of nominated,
but you never
expect something like that.
And in the morning, I was just
getting on with my normal work.
Actually, my wife was
working at home, as well,
and it got to about 10:30.
And we thought,
oh, obviously, it's
not happened this year,
because we hadn't heard.
And then, suddenly,
my wife's computer
started ringing on her
Skype, I think it was.
And I said, what's
that annoying noise?
And then it turned out
to be a call from Sweden,
and they were desperately
trying to get hold of my number,
and they didn't have
John's number either.
So everyone was in
a kind of panic,
but it all added to the
drama of the moment.
Just 10 minutes before--
HANNAH FRY: You
had john's number?
DEMIS HASSABIS: Yes, exactly.
Just before it
was all announced.
HANNAH FRY: Absolutely
amazing, and I also
saw that you celebrated
by having a poker
night with some chess
champions, including Magnus
Carlsen and Hikaru, as well.
I want to know, who won?
What's your bluffing strategy?
[LAUGHING]
DEMIS HASSABIS: Well, it turned
out-- yeah, so that happened.
I think it was on the
Wednesday, and it turned out
that there was a big, huge chess
tournament going on in London.
And one of my chess
friends, old chess friends
from when I was young, was
hosting a poker and chess
evening the day after.
So I thought it was the
perfect way to celebrate,
but I don't recommend
a home poker game
with a couple of
world poker champions
and a couple of
ex-world chess champions
if you want to win some money.
But that's my idea of
fun, and it was actually
an amazing night.
I know it sounds pretty nerdy,
but it was actually my idea
of heaven to celebrate it.
HANNAH FRY: Not at all.
It sounds like--
DEMIS HASSABIS: Yeah, I can't
tell you the bluffing strategy.
Otherwise, I'll never
beat Magnus again.
[LAUGHING]
Yeah.
HANNAH FRY: Well, this is true.
This is true.
I mean, aside from
the Nobel Prize,
a sentence that no one has
ever said, before that,
in advance of that, you also
were named as a citation
laureates, because the AlphaFold
work has now been cited over
28,000 times, which
is incredible.
I mean, this is really not very
long ago that it came through.
Are there standout applications
for you that really resonate?
DEMIS HASSABIS: Well, I mean,
it is mind boggling, really,
and it's everything
we hoped would happen
by putting AlphaFold out
there, and open sourcing
it, and putting it out
with the community,
with our fantastic partners at
EMBL-EBI, like Janet, and Ewan,
and the audience as well, to
get it out there to everyone.
I mean, there's so many
applications to mention,
but I think maybe I can mention
two or three of my favorites
were kind of determining the
structure of the nuclear pore
complex, one of the most
important and largest proteins
in the human body.
And it's really
important, because it
governs the molecules
and nutrients that go in
and out of your cell nucleus.
And, actually, several teams
used AlphaFold predictions,
as well as their own
experimental data,
to finally piece that
very, very complex protein
structure together.
So that was amazing.
Another piece of work
that I really like
is [INAUDIBLE] work
from the [? Broad, ?]
developing a molecular syringe
to deliver drug payloads
to hard parts of
the body to reach,
and he also used
AlphaFold to help
modify how that molecular
syringe was designed.
And then maybe, finally,
my other favorite project
is from the University of
Portsmouth, John McKeon's group,
designing plastic eating
enzymes using AlphaFold.
But I think it's
just a small sample
of all the incredible work
researchers are doing with it.
HANNAH FRY: But these
kind of projects, I
mean, these are your
favorites, right?
The ones where it's not just
sort of the technical idea
itself, it's the
potential impact
that it can have
further downstream?
DEMIS HASSABIS:
Yeah, that's right.
I mean, that's why I was always
intrigued by the protein folding
problem or protein structure
prediction problem was because I
felt-- we sometimes call it a
deepmind, a root node problem.
What we mean by that
is, if you think
of the whole tree
of knowledge, there
are certain problems, where if
there are root node problems,
if you unlock them, if you
discover a solution to them,
it would unlock a whole new
branch or avenue of discovery.
And I always felt that
determining a protein structure
in this way would do that,
lead to disease understanding,
drug design, and
much more, and that
seems to be what's happened.
HANNAH FRY: OK, so not to
be greedy, but what's next?
Is there another AlphaFold?
I mean, GNoME is a
personal favorite of mine.
Maybe tell us a little
bit about that project.
DEMIS HASSABIS: Yes, so GNoME,
look, there's so many areas,
and actually, James
mentioned it in the morning,
and Pushmeet talked
about it as well,
that we're touching on
nearly every area of science.
And GNoME is one of my
favorite projects, which
is on material
design, and I think
material design has some of the
same characteristics that we
look for in a problem
that's suitable for AI.
It's massive
combinatorial space.
You need to try and
build a model that
understands the physics
and the chemistry
of the natural phenomena.
And then, if you have
a model like that,
you can then maybe use it to
do a very efficient search
through that search space,
combinatorial space,
and then find an
optimal solution.
And in materials,
I think that would
be also just as groundbreaking.
You could imagine designing new
batteries or maybe, one day,
discovering a room
temperature superconductor.
It has always been
one of my dreams.
So I think we're, obviously,
at the early stages of that.
I would characterize it as sort
of AlphaFold one level maybe,
and we've got to
get to AlphaFold
two level of predictions.
But we can see a
clear path from there,
and GNoME is the
beginning of that work.
We published it in--
I think it was in
Science, last year,
and we discovered 200,000 new
crystals that no one had ever
seen before.
So I think that shows some of
the potential of AI in things,
like material design.
And then maybe the other
thing I'm excited about
is AI applied to
mathematics and, perhaps,
solving one of the
great conjectures, maybe
one of the Millennium
prize problems,
and using AI as a big
part of that solution.
HANNAH FRY: So that idea,
though, about GNoME,
also about AlphaFold, is
you're sort of shortcutting,
or you're making the
sort of synthetic world
really serve your advantage.
So you're not having
to do everything
through experimentation.
I know that you
and Paul Nurse have
been talking about a virtual
cell for a long while.
Tell us a little bit about that.
DEMIS HASSABIS: Well, Paul
has been one of my mentors.
He's very generously mentored
me in the life sciences
for more than 25 years
now or something,
and it's been incredible,
sort of inspiring,
talking to Paul very
regularly about these topics.
And, unusually, I think Paul for
a life scientist, a biologist,
he's thought a lot about biology
as an information system.
He's written a lot of
interesting research papers
on that.
So we've always had this
discussion in the background,
and I've always wondered-- every
five years or so, I've thought,
do we have enough
technologies, enough
know how to actually
really attempt this Mount
Everest of a problem
or trying to build
a virtual cell, a simulation
of a cell, basically,
that would be predictive
of something that's
really going to happen?
And every five years, I
thought, we don't really
have enough technologies yet.
But, finally, now, I think the
answer is, yes, we probably
do have enough know how and
techniques that we could attempt
this seriously now.
And maybe, in the
next five to 10 years,
we could eventually
build up a picture
of a virtual cell, perhaps, a
yeast cell to start off with,
as Paul's always been working on
as the model organism for this.
And my way of thinking
about it is you
can think of AlphaFold2
as essentially
solving the static picture
of what a protein looks like.
But of course, we know
biology is a dynamic system.
That's where all the interesting
things happen in biology,
and AlphaFold3 is our
first step towards trying
to model those interactions.
So AlphaFold3 can model
pairwise interactions
between proteins and proteins,
proteins and RNA, proteins,
and DNA.
And then maybe the
next step up from that
would be to model
a whole pathway,
and then, eventually, maybe we
can make it to an entire cell.
HANNAH FRY: Amazing.
I mean, I imagine that the
advent of quantum computing
will make a difference now that
it's possible to do simulations
down at the molecular level.
DEMIS HASSABIS: Yeah, so
look, quantum computing
is very exciting.
It itself is accelerating,
and James also
mentioned earlier
this morning about
the interesting
cross-pollination
that's happening between
AI and quantum computing.
Actually, we collaborate a
lot with our quantum computing
group at Google, which is one
of the world's best quantum
computing teams, on things,
like error correction codes
and things like that.
And of course, one of the
uses of a quantum computer
would be to simulate quantum
systems, like molecules,
and atoms, and things
like that, and compounds,
and then produce a lot of
synthetic data potentially.
But, interestingly, I also have
a slightly controversial take,
which is--
and I've talked to some of the
world's top quantum computer
scientists on this,
is that, actually, I
believe that classical Turing
machines, classical computers,
are capable of a lot more
than we previously thought.
And I think that's what the
work that we've been doing
has shown, both with
AlphaFold, but also
previously with AlphaGo,
our program that
beat the world champion
at the game of Go, which
just as an indication
of the complexity
Go, much more
complicated than chess
and has more possible
board positions
than there are atoms in the
universe, 10 to the power 170.
So what that means
is you can't possibly
brute force a solution
to find the best
move in a particular position.
You have to do
something much cleverer.
So both protein folding
also has enormous numbers
of possibilities, if you were
to do it naively and just try
every combination, which is
the naive way of doing it.
But yeah, if you
do things, like you
do a huge amount of pre-compute
to build a model of the system
before you ask it the question
that you're interested in,
what move should I play
in this go position,
how does this novel
protein fold, it turns out
you can actually come back
with a near optimal move in Go
in a few seconds or fold a
protein in a matter of minutes,
which you might have
expected in protein space,
at least, that you might need a
quantum computer for or quantum
algorithm for.
And it turns out, you don't.
So I actually think we should
take this very seriously,
that classical systems, if
you use them in the right way,
may be capable of modeling
a lot more complex systems,
perhaps, even
counterintuitively,
quantum systems.
Because, normally, you talk
about needing quantum computers
to model any kind
of classical system,
but it may be that
classical systems can
model quantum systems.
And I've tested this out
with some of the people,
like Professor Zeilinger, who
won the Nobel Prize in physics
recently for his pioneering
quantum computing work,
and he thought it
was very interesting.
And David Deutsch, also,
one of my scientific heroes,
who's basically invented quantum
computing, said it was crazy,
but the right sort of crazy,
which coming from him,
I take as a compliment
and as also a sign
to maybe pursue it further.
HANNAH FRY: So let me make
sure I understand this then.
So, I guess, the
classical computers
are thought of as
deterministic machines,
and you're talking here about
probabilistic questions.
But you can use the
deterministic ones.
DEMIS HASSABIS:
Yeah, and the idea
is that quantum systems
are anything you try
to mold, any natural phenomena.
If you start trying to model
it by every possibility
that it could take, that
system, then you quickly
run out of computation
in a classical system.
You need too many
bits to model it,
but that's just not the way
that you would attempt to do it
with a classical system.
You would build a model,
first, that learns--
my conjecture on it would be
that any natural phenomena
tends to have structure.
And if it has structure,
you could potentially
learn it with a classical
machine learning system,
learn an efficient
model of that,
and then use that to
search the possibilities
in an efficient way.
So I think that might get around
some of the inefficiencies
from a naive way of doing this.
I mean, it's a pretty big claim,
so I'm making it in a soft way.
But it's a kind of hobby of
mine to look at this area,
and it's something, I think,
that could be quite promising.
HANNAH FRY: Another
hobby of yours
that has now spun off into
its own business, Isomorphic,
because, I mean, this
is work using AlphaFold
to apply to drug discovery.
DEMIS HASSABIS: Yes.
HANNAH FRY: You've got some
pretty prestigious partnerships
now as well.
Can you tell us what Isomorphic
is focusing on at the moment?
DEMIS HASSABIS: Yeah, so
Isomorphic is our spin out
to try and revolutionize drug
discovery from first principles
using AI from the ground up,
trying to re-imagine the drug
discovery process.
And I had this in mind when
we were doing AlphaFold.
So I think it's one of the most,
obviously, good use cases of AI
is to cure diseases.
I mean, what better use
of AI could there be?
So that has always been one
of the number one things
I wanted to do with AI
once it got mature enough.
So AlphaFold, of
course, is a great tool
for fundamental research and
fundamental biology research.
Over two million
researchers around the world
have used AlphaFold and the
structures we put out there,
but it's also a practical use
to help with drug discovery.
Of course, knowing the
structure of a protein
is only one small part of the
whole drug discovery process.
So we spun out isomorphic
after we did AlphaFold two
to build on that work and extend
and build new machine learning
systems into adjacent
areas, things,
like designing chemical
compounds and drug compounds,
testing for toxicity
and predicting things,
like ADME properties,
important properties
that you need for drugs
to work in the body
and minimize side effects
and things like that.
And we're building-- you can
think of it as building up
further AlphaFold-like models
in these adjacent areas.
And then, eventually, we'll
stick them all together,
and we hope that one day, in
the near future, actually,
we'll reduce the time down
from years, maybe even decade,
to design a drug down to
months or perhaps even weeks.
And that would
revolutionize, I think,
the drug discovery process.
HANNAH FRY: This
really does seem
like one of those
endeavors, where you're not
having to make a choice between
doing something that benefits
humanity and doing
something that can be
profitable and sustain itself.
How much do you look for those
kind of projects at the outset,
or how much of it is that you
do sort of blue sky research
and then hope that it'll
turn out that way in the end?
DEMIS HASSABIS: I
think we do both.
We're research led as a
group, always have been,
so we try to do what's the right
next step for researching AGI,
Artificial General Intelligence,
or something, like AlphaFold,
where you really want to solve
the scientific challenge.
But then, in the back of my
mind, I'm also pretty practical.
So I want to solve
things that will also
have a practical, positive
impact on the world
pretty directly.
So if you can find
projects that do both,
then that's the
holy grail, really,
and something that
really worthwhile,
investing a lot of
time and effort.
And AlphaFold was
exactly like that.
So I was fascinated
by the problem itself.
As Janet mentioned earlier,
the more you get into proteins,
they're exquisite
bionanomachines.
I mean, they're
unbelievably beautiful.
I totally agree
with Janet on that,
and you sort of fall
in love with them
when you start working on them,
and it's just unbelievable
what nature's engineered.
But then I always had in mind
that would be a social good,
but then Isomorphic
can do both, right?
I think we can cure many
diseases with the help of AI,
but also, it should be an
incredibly valuable company.
And that, of course, if that
turns out to be the case,
that will give us more money to
invest in fundamental research.
So it's all a virtuous cycle.
HANNAH FRY: With all of
these different projects
going on in
fundamental research,
I mean, do you think that we
are, at this moment, where we're
going to have sort of a
vertical takeoff, as it were,
on progress?
DEMIS HASSABIS: I think we
are on the cusp of that.
I really do feel like we're on
the brink of a new golden era
of discovery, like the whole
of today's symposium is called.
And I think what we
need is a lot more
interdisciplinary science,
so using AI, bringing
in the right way.
We're asking the right
questions with domain experts,
and I think it's
almost limitless,
what its applications could be.
And of course, AI itself
is a scientific discipline,
is improving all the time.
So there's applying today's
technologies directly
to the other fields,
and then there's also
continuing to improve AI
itself, and that as well
is a sort of
exponential improvement.
So there's a lot of,
I think, progress
to be made in just
the next few years.
HANNAH FRY: I think
interdisciplinarity
has definitely been one of
the big themes of today,
but I think that the other
themes are about what science
looks like in the future.
What does it mean to do
science in an era of AI?
And I know that you're
a sort of big advocate
for the scientific
method, but just talk
to me a little bit about that.
What does that look like
as we kind of move forward?
DEMIS HASSABIS: Well, look,
I think the scientific method
is arguably maybe the greatest
idea humans have ever had
and, I think, underpins,
obviously, all
of science, but then
also because of that,
science and technology,
modern civilization.
And I think more than ever,
we need to anchor around
that method in today's world,
and I think, especially
with something as powerful
and potentially transformative
as AI, I think it's important we
use more the scientific method
than, perhaps, lean on
normal sort of technology,
A/B testing out in the wild.
That is often the case
with new technologies,
and I feel we should treat this
more as a scientific endeavor,
if possible,
although, it obviously
has all the implications
that breakthrough
technologies normally have in
terms of the speed of adoption
and the speed of change.
So it's an interesting
situation that we're in,
and I think we need to use the
scientific method to do things,
like better understand what
these systems do, build
benchmarks and
rigorous evaluations
for understanding the
limits of the capabilities,
interpretability.
Actually, I think we should be
using neuroscience techniques,
which are, obviously, built
for understanding real brains,
to model virtual brains.
I sometimes call it
virtual brain analysis.
What's the equivalent of an fMRI
of these neural networks, right?
And I think there's a lot we
can learn, and bring across,
and translate from
the natural sciences
to what is basically
an engineering science.
I call it an
engineering science,
because unlike the
natural sciences,
you have to build the
artifact of interest, first,
and then, once you
have it, you can then
use the scientific method to
reduce it down and understand
its components.
So it's a whole challenge.
That's for sure, and these
systems, in my opinion,
are as complex as the
natural phenomena we normally
want to study.
So no one should think
that it would be easier
to understand one of
these artificial systems
than it would be to
understand a natural system.
I think they're just as
complex in many ways.
So we've got a lot of
work in front of us,
and I think this is something
that industry, academia,
and civil society needs to come
together to understand better,
including how to deploy
these technologies.
HANNAH FRY: Where does
a scientist's intuition
fit in all of this?
DEMIS HASSABIS: Well, I
think scientist's intuition
and creativity is critical.
I think, right now, the
AI systems are just tools.
I think they're great
for finding correlations,
and patterns, and structures
in data, but for the moment,
they're not able to come up
with their own hypotheses
or their own questions.
And I think, as all the
scientists in the room know,
I think that's the hardest
thing about science
is asking the right
question, and that
has to still come
from human scientists.
And for the foreseeable future,
I think that will be the case.
So I think it could
be-- that's why
I'm very excited about the
use of these AI systems
as maybe the ultimate
tools to help us accelerate
scientific discovery.
HANNAH FRY: I guess on that
topic of humans, I mean,
there has been some
recent research saying
that the progress of
scientific discovery
has slowed down
over recent years.
What's your take
on that, and what
could be done to change that?
DEMIS HASSABIS: Well,
look, I think things--
I've seen studies
like that, too,
and I think there's interesting
conjectures as to why that is.
Science has become
a bigger endeavor.
You need to have bigger teams,
more expensive equipment,
and so on.
So that leads to some slowdown,
and perhaps, the questions
we're now tackling
are ever more complex.
So that's also tricky.
I think what I
would recommend is,
again, I think, a lot of the
advances in the next 10 years
are going to be
interdisciplinary work, sort
of bringing together experts
from two or more fields,
and then that making the big
advances in between those areas.
And, actually, that's the story
of DeepMind, which originally, I
think, was a combination
of neuroscience ideas
with machine learning ideas.
It's also the
story of AlphaFold,
which is the combination
of biologists and chemists
on our team, along with machine
learning experts and engineers.
So I've always found
that's the place
to get the most
advances most quickly,
and actually, we're announcing
today, a $20 million
fund from Google.org
to fund this kind
of interdisciplinary
work in academia.
And I hope that other
funders join that effort,
and I think that's
what we need is
to train a new generation
of PhDs and postdocs
in these kind of combination
of these different areas.
HANNAH FRY: What an
extraordinary piece of news
to finish on, $20
million for research
in interdisciplinary teams.
Demis, thank you
so much, indeed.
Thank you very much.
[APPLAUSE]
OK, I think you go down to
join the other laureates,
while we out the stage.
Now, I should tell
you, actually,
the laureates, the word,
laureates, by the way,
comes from the
laurel wreath, which
was given to victors in ancient
Greece as a sign of honor.
And every year, there
are five separate prizes
that are awarded to those who
are considered to have conferred
the greatest benefit to
humankind courtesy of Alfred
Nobel.
There is a rumor that there is
no Nobel Prize for mathematics,
because Alfred Nobel's
great love ran away
with a mathematician.
Have we heard this?
I mean, we are charming.
What can I say?
Now, so joining me
on the stage, we
have three further recipients
of this esteemed prize,
John Jumper.
We've not yet had the
pleasure of having John
Jumper on the stage thus far.
He is a director at
Google DeepMind, where
he led the team that built
AlphaFold and continues
to work on new methods
to apply machine learning
to protein biology.
John has won numerous
awards for his work,
including the Lasker
Award, the Breakthrough
Prize in Life Sciences, the
Canada International Award,
and of course, this year's
Nobel Prize in chemistry.
We are also joined
by Sir Paul Nurse,
who is CEO of the
Francis Crick Institute
and winner of the 2001 prize
in physiology of medicine
for his work also on proteins,
specifically the protein
molecules that control the
division of cells in the cell
cycle.

Thank you.
OK, so I know that,
often, you get

were when you heard,
what were you doing

I want to ask you a
slightly different question.
I want to ask you about

work that you were doing
was genuinely groundbreaking.
Was there sort of a
moment when you realized
the significance of it?
John?
JOHN JUMPER: I think there were
kind of two moments for me.
One was actually
watching Twitter
after-- whenever you release
some work into the world,
you anxiously refresh
Twitter, and we
stick to the word AlphaFold, and
just see what tweets popped up.
And I remember seeing,
when the database
became available, so many
astounded grad students saying--
I think one was, how did
they get my structure,
it hasn't been published,
how in the world,
and just had
absolute astounding.
Like, the number of
people that said either
we predicted their structure,
this person down the hall,
we predicted their structure, so
I thought they were absolutely
blown away.
And I think the
second moment was
there was a special
issue of Science related
to the structure of the
nuclear pore, the largest
collection of proteins in the
human cell, protein chains,
and there had been, like,
four papers in a special issue
of Science, and
three out of the four
had made huge
usage of AlphaFold.
Something like more
than 100 mentions
of the word
AlphaFold in Science,
and we had nothing
to do with it.
We didn't know it was happening.
It just appeared
one day, and that
was the moment when I really
knew that people were doing
science worthy of appearing
in Science, one of the most
prestigious journals in the
world, on top of our tools
and without us.
And the moment at which people
start making these discoveries
on top of what
we've built, that's
when you really make it as
someone who makes tools.
HANNAH FRY: Jennifer,
did you have a moment?
JENNIFER DOUDNA:
Well, I think for us,
yeah, maybe also two moments.
One was in the Fall
of probably 2011, when
we had started a collaboration
with Emmanuelle Charpentier
to work on CRISPR, which is
a bacterial immune system,
and we wondered,
how does it work?
And we had together figured
out that it's an RNA guided
system that targets
DNA for cutting,
for cleavage, and furthermore,
that we could actually program
the system, because
we understood
the chemistry of how it worked.
And it was really one
of those aha moments.
Personally, I was just astounded
that bacteria had figured out
how to do this and that,
furthermore, we now understood
how to harness that system to
manipulate DNA in new ways.
So that was one, and
then the second one
was sort of akin
to what you said,
John, where I think it was then
fast forwarding about a year
or so.
We published that work in the
Summer of 2012, and by the Fall,
I was starting to get
emails from people
all over the world
who had read the paper
and said, "Oh, my gosh,
this is so exciting.
I'm now using it to test
genes in Drosophila.
I'm starting to test
genes in zebrafish.
I'm excited about what
I'm doing in human cells."
I was getting messages from
people that I often barely knew
who were just
very, very excited,
and you could start to
feel the momentum that
was building in the field.
SIR PAUL NURSE: Cool.
Well, I'm the oldie here,
so it's 1985, I'm afraid,
and I work on yeast, as
you've already been told,
and my lab had worked
out the genes that
controlled the cell cycle.
That is the process that leads
to the reproduction of one cell
into two fundamental to growth
and development in all living
organisms, and we'd
identified that gene.
But the blunt thing is
that, who cares about yeast?
I mean, being perfectly
honest, I care about yeast,
but most of the world
does not care about yeast.
So I found our work
wasn't really being
taken much notice of.
So I thought, do humans
have the same gene?
Now, this is long before the
human genome sequence and so on.
So we did a crazy experiment.
I want to just tell
you how crazy it was.
We had a mutant in yeast
that couldn't grow,
because it was
defective in this gene.
We took the first
human cDNA library.
It's genES that
had ever been made.
We didn't make it.
We got it a few months
afterwards and fundamentally
sprinkled the genes onto
the defective yeast,
arguing that, if there
was a gene in humans that
could do the same job as
the defective gene in yeast,
if the yeast cell took it
up, if it could be expressed,
if it worked, then those
cells would grow and divide,
and we could get the gene back
and show that humans had it.
That experiment had
no right to work.
Yeast, humans probably diverged
1,500 million years ago,
and we were demanding that it
still worked after 1,500 million
years, and you
know, it did work.
When we got that result--
and by the way,
there were a couple
of months when we had
to sequence the genes.
It took that long.
Then I used to
think, I'm just going
to go home and
believe this worked.
Tomorrow, I will go
into work, and we
will have shown it didn't
work, but it did work.
And that's when I
thought, perhaps, it
might be recognized,
because it wasn't yeast.
It was humans.
